proposals.title: Filter Stage Elimination Causing Zero-Row Output
proposals.detect: rows_actual == 0 with rows_expected > 0, AND the corresponding dim_filter phase timing collapses to ~0 ms (from a normal non-zero value in a previous passing iteration)
proposals.root_cause: Q3 iter_1: optimizer removed or replaced the filter predicate with an always-false condition (possibly removed the filter stage entirely or flipped a comparison operator). dim_filter went from 9.8 ms to 0 ms; output dropped from 10 rows to 0 rows.
proposals.fix: Treat rows_actual == 0 with rows_expected > 0 as a definitive correctness failure regardless of other metrics. When dim_filter timing collapses to 0 ms from a prior non-zero value, the filter predicate was incorrectly eliminated — revert to the previous passing iteration's filter logic. Never accept a zero-row result as a performance win.

proposals.title: Join Restructuring Regression — Correct Row Count, Wrong Row Values
proposals.detect: rows_actual == rows_expected but column_failures exist across join key, aggregated value, and date columns simultaneously. main_scan timing is 5-20x higher than the best passing iteration. build_joins timing also increases significantly.
proposals.root_cause: Q3 iter_5: optimizer restructured the join or probe side (build_joins 23 ms → 87 ms, main_scan 66 ms → 1,103 ms). The new join produced incorrect row associations — wrong l_orderkey, revenue, and o_orderdate — even though the row count happened to match. Suspect wrong composite join key or hash table overwrite (C15 variant).
proposals.fix: When an optimization modifies the join build/probe structure and produces correct row count but wrong values, suspect the join key is missing a column or the probe-side filter changed. Treat any column_failures across both the join key column and aggregated value as a join logic regression. Revert build_joins and main_scan logic to the last passing iteration.

proposals.title: Thread-Local Aggregation Merge Using std::unordered_map — O(n*t) Sequential Merge
proposals.detect: aggregation_merge phase consumes >500 ms in a multi-threaded query with GROUP BY. Per-thread partial results are stored in std::unordered_map<Key, Value> and then merged by iterating each thread's map and inserting into a global map.
proposals.root_cause: Q3 iter_0: aggregation_merge took 3,263 ms (38% of total runtime). Each thread accumulated partial GROUP BY results in a separate unordered_map; the merge phase iterated all t thread maps sequentially. With 60M LINEITEM rows and many unique (orderkey, orderdate, shippriority) groups, the maps were large and merge was expensive.
proposals.fix: Use a single shared open-addressing hash table with thread-safe atomic CAS updates, OR size thread-local flat arrays keyed by a dense integer range and reduce with a parallel prefix sum. For Q3-class GROUP BY (cardinality ~O(millions)): pre-allocate one global hash table and use per-slot spinlocks or lock-free atomics. After this fix, aggregation_merge dropped from 3,263 ms to 22 ms.

proposals.title: data_loading Dominates Unoptimized Queries — Missed madvise Opportunity
proposals.detect: Query has zero optimization iterations AND data_loading phase accounts for >50% of total hot runtime. Relevant for queries with large column files (LINEITEM, ORDERS at SF10: 60M+ rows).
proposals.root_cause: Q1 (data_loading 48 ms / 88 ms = 55%) and Q9 (data_loading 122 ms / 187 ms = 65%) both had no optimization triggered despite data_loading being the dominant phase. The optimizer threshold for triggering optimization may not account for I/O-bound queries where the first pass is already 'fast enough' by time budget, yet data_loading is the clear bottleneck.
proposals.fix: For any query where data_loading exceeds 40% of total hot runtime, always attempt at least one optimization iteration targeting I/O: (1) add zone-map-guided selective madvise(MADV_WILLNEED) on qualifying byte ranges only (P13); (2) ensure GENDB_PHASE('data_loading') is the first phase so I/O is measured separately (P14). Even if absolute timing is acceptable, reducing data_loading unlocks further parallelism.

proposals.title: Reinforce P12: Strict Revert After Single Regression — Q3 and Q18 Evidence
proposals.detect:
Two queries in this run exhibited multi-step oscillation after reaching best:
  - Q3: best at iter_3 (149 ms) → iter_4 regresses to 215 ms (+44%) → iter_5 fails with wrong results
  - Q18: best at iter_1 (612 ms) → iter_2 crashes → iter_3 regresses to 775 ms (+27%) → iter_4 recovers at 513 ms
In both cases the optimizer continued past its own best by at least 2 additional iterations.
proposals.fix: Enforce: if iteration N+1 is >15% worse than the current best-validated timing, immediately revert to best-known-good source and switch optimization direction. Do NOT emit iter_N+2 from a regressed baseline — always branch from the best-known-good. In Q3, this would have stopped at iter_3 (149 ms) and avoided the iter_5 failure entirely. In Q18, reverting after iter_3 regression would have started iter_4 from iter_1's source.

proposals.title: Reinforce C23: Hash Table Crash Recurred in Q18 iter_2 for Large Subquery
proposals.detect: Q18 iter_2: process killed by signal after 2 s, zero [TIMING] lines in stdout — exactly the C23 pattern. The optimizer introduced a new hash join structure for the LINEITEM subquery (which aggregates ~60M rows at SF10). The hash table was under-sized for the full cardinality.
proposals.root_cause: Q18 contains a correlated subquery: SELECT l_orderkey FROM lineitem GROUP BY l_orderkey HAVING SUM(l_quantity) > 300. This produces ~50K qualifying orderkeys at SF10. Any hash table built for this must be sized for the full LINEITEM orderkey domain (~15M distinct orderkeys), not just the qualifying set. Under-sizing causes 100% load factor and infinite probing.
proposals.fix: For Q18-class subqueries (GROUP BY over LINEITEM with HAVING threshold), always size the hash table for the FULL input cardinality before filtering: capacity = next_power_of_2(2 * 15_000_000) = 33_554_432. Use std::fill for EMPTY_KEY initialization (not memset). This is a specific instantiation of C9+C20 for the Q18 pattern.
